fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the weirder one. Whenever I say something like "Every BLAH is a SUCH-AND-SUCH", I always mean to include the case where there are no BLAHs at all. Again, I won"t bother with any special qualifications; you can always take this for granted. So, for example, I"m perfectly happy to say: "All unicorns breathe fire". Since there are no unicorns, my "all" claim is all good.

There are a few logicians' habits you should know about, to be able to make the most of these lectures and our conversations. These are habits of expression that are completely standard among logicians, but can throw you off if you're not used to them. I'm flagging these habits here so that you can be ready. I'll try to remember to flag them as well where I use them, but these are so ingrained in me by now that it's entirely likely I won't notice some.

If you notice some that I haven't flagged, let me know! That way, I can correct the lectures. The same goes for any typos or errors of any sort, or recommendations in general. These lectures get revised every time the unit runs; they've improved a lot (to their present ok-but-not-exactly-gonna-change-the-world state) as a result of past students' input. I really appreciate their input, and I'll really appreciate yours!

Whenever I say something like "BLAH or SUCH-AND-SUCH", I always mean to include the case where both BLAH and SUCH-AND-SUCH happen. I won't bother with any extra qualification like "or both", or anything like that. You can always take for granted that I mean to include the both case. So, for example, I'd be perfectly happy to say: "Melbourne is in Victoria or Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun". Both of these are true, so my "or" claim is all good.

This is the weirder one. Whenever I say something like "Every BLAH is a SUCH-AND-SUCH", I always mean to include the case where there are no BLAHs at all. Again, I won"t bother with any special qualifications; you can always take this for granted. So, for example, I"m perfectly happy to say: "All unicorns breathe fire". Since there are no unicorns, my "all" claim is all good.

For both of these, there's a case to be made that what I'm doing is out of step with what "or" or "all" mean in English (Note the use of Habit 1 there!), and there's a case to be made that it isn't. But for our purposes, it's not important to settle that question. What is important is that you see precisely what I mean when I say "or" or "all", and keeping these logicians' habits in mind is key for that.

As a warmup, say that a Boolean valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A when every premise in \Gamma gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuation v and the conclusion A gets value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on v. If an argument has any counterexamples, then we can see that the truth of its premises does not guarantee the truth of its conclusion---the counterexample shows a way to distribute truth values among the atomic formulas that makes the premises all true without making the conclusion true.

So we can get a logic from our Boolean valuations like this: if any Boolean valuation is a counterexample to an argument, this logic counts the argument as invalid. On the other hand, if no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to the argument, then this logic counts the argument as valid. This is a very widely used logic, called classical logic.

Recall the history here, though: this only goes back to the 19th century. So the "classical" here is more like the "classical" in "classical music" than it is like the "classical" in "classical architecture".

Let's return to some earlier example arguments, to see how they fare in classical logic. First, think about p \land q \;/\; p. To see whether this argument is classically valid or not, we should ask: is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? Remember, a counterexample to this argument is a valuation v where v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By looking at the Boolean structure, though, you can see that this is impossible: if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then no matter what v(q) is we also have v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to p \land q \;/\; p. As a result, this argument is classically valid.

For a contrasting example, think about p \lor q \;/\; p. Is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? A counterexample to this argument would be a valuation w where w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Is there any such w? Yes, there is. Since there is always a valuation for any combination of values for our atomic sentences, consider any valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. In any such valuation, we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so any such valuation is a counterexample to this argument. As a result, this argument is not classically valid.

Just like the Boolean structure, classical logic doesn't have to be interpreted as about truth. Recall that if we're thinking about electrical circuits, each sentence represents a component of the circuit that can be on or off. In this case, when an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is classically valid, it means that whenever all the components represented by sentences in \Gamma are on, then the component represented by A must also be on. A counterexample to the argument would be some way of setting things up so that all the premise components are on and the conclusion component is off.

What about arguments \Gamma \;/\; A where \Gamma is the empty set of premises? Remember, we can have arguments with no premises. What does it take for a valuation to be a counterexample to one of these? The same thing it takes to be a counterexample to any argument: it has to make all the premises take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the conclusion take value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. To interpret this, we need to remember logicians' weird habit 2: if there are no Xs, then "all Xs are Ys" automatically counts as true. So when there are no premises, "all the premises take value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on this valuation" is always true, no matter what valuation we're looking at. That means that, for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument \;/\; A with no premises, all it needs to do is take the sentence A to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The none premises are automatic.

That means that an argument \;/\; A is classically valid whenever the sentence A takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on every Boolean valuation. Such sentences are called theorems. Just like "valid" or "invalid", "theorem" is always relative to a logic. A sentence A might be a theorem in one logic but not another, because the argument \;/\; A is valid in the first logic but not the other. Indeed, we'll see examples like that soon.
A theorem of a logic is a sentence A such that the argument \;/\; A is valid in the logic.

One notable theorem of classical logic is p \lor 
eg p. In setting up our Boolean valuations, we forced every sentence to be true or false on each valuation. Since negation takes falsity to truth, on every valuation one or the other of p and 
eg p is true. And a disjunction is true when at least one of the disjuncts is true. So p \lor 
eg p comes out true no matter what value p has. This appears in the resulting logic: \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is a valid argument.

In fact, we can go farther. For any sentence A, the sentence A \lor 
eg A is a classical theorem. Make sure you see how to interpret this. There's no particular sentence "A \lor 
eg A" in our language; A isn't part of the language at all. Rather, what I'm saying is that p \lor 
eg p is a classical theorem, and q \lor 
eg q is a classical theorem, and (p \land q) \lor 
eg (p \land q) is a classical theorem, and 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) \lor 
eg 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) is a classical theorem, and so on. I'm using A as a variable, to make a claim about lots of different sentences all at once.

Before we move on, let's look at one more example. Think about the argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q. Is this classically valid? To find out, we need to think whether it has any classical counterexamples. A classical counterexample would be a Boolean valuation v where v(p) = v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since it would need to make both premises true and the conclusion not true. But this can't happen; if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) can't also be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, because of how negation is handled in Boolean valuations. So there are no classical counterexamples to p, 
eg p \;/\; q. This argument is classically valid.

That sometimes can throw people for a loop; it seems like p and \neg p have nothing to do with q! So how can the argument be valid? This is where it's important to remember how logic works: we lay out precise and clear definitions, and we see where they follow. The way we've set up classical logic, it just falls out that p, \neg p \;/\; q is valid. If it seems to you like arguments like this shouldn't be valid, that might give you a reason to explore logics other than classical logic. This is one of the ways different logics will differ from each other.
The most important kind of relationship between sets is the subset relation.
A set \Gamma is a subset of a set \Delta iff: every member of \Gamma is also a member of \Delta . There's a symbol for this: \Gamma \subseteq \Delta means that \Gamma is a subset of \Delta .

In the above definition, \Gamma and \Delta are capital Greek letters: \Gamma is Gamma and \Delta is Delta. It's common to use lots of different alphabets in logic, with the Greek alphabet probably the most common one other than our own Latin alphabet. I'll use capital Greek letters like these particularly for sets of things, usually sets of formulas.

When \Gamma \subseteq \Delta , we know that everything in \Gamma is in \Delta as well. There might be some other things also in \Delta as well, or there might not; that doesn't matter. (If there isn't anything else in \Delta , then by extensionality \Gamma and \Delta must be the same set.)

Some things to note about subsets:
There is an important difference between being a subset of a set and being a member of the set. For example, \{p, q\} is a subset of \{p, q, r\}, since every member of the former is also a member of the latter. But \{p, q\} is not a member of \{p, q, r\}; only p, q, and r are members of this set.
Every set is a subset of itself. For example, consider the set \{p, q \land r\}. This has two members: p and q \land r. And both of these members are indeed also members of \{p, q \land r\}, so the definition of subsethood is met. More generally, for any set \Gamma , all the members of \Gamma are indeed members of \Gamma , so \Gamma is always a subset of itself, for any \Gamma .
The empty set is a subset of absolutely any set. This might be a surprise, but remember logicians' weird habit number 2! To check whether the empty set is a subset of some set \Gamma , we need to check whether all the members of the empty set are also members of \Gamma . Since there are no members of the empty set, indeed it is true that all the members of the empty set are members of \Gamma ---no matter what \Gamma is. (Using logicians' weird habit number 2, it's even true that all the members of the empty set are square circles, that they're all plants with mammal-like brains, and that they're all behind you right now.)

fr:title.text: Method 1: preserve the main connective, apply to components

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Method 1: preserve the main connective, apply to components

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a sentence and a substitution, if the sentence is atomic we already know how to apply the substitution to it. But what if the sentence is compound? Then the result we're after is this: we preserve the main connective, and apply the substitution to each of the components.

So, for example, take our substitution h from above. What is h(q \rightarrow r)? This is compound, so we want to preserve the main connective \rightarrow , and apply the substitution to each of the components: our result should be h(q) \rightarrow h(r). And what is that? Well, both q and r are atomic, so we know how to apply h to them: h(q) = p \land q and h(r) = q. So h(q \rightarrow r) = h(q) \rightarrow h(r) = (p \land q) \rightarrow q. (Note the parentheses!)

For a more complex example, think about h(p \land (q \lor \bot )). This is compound, so we want to keep the main connective as it is and apply h to the components. This gets us to h(p) \land h(q \lor \bot ). Now, h(p) is good to go, since we know h(p) = q. So our result should be q \land h(q \lor \bot ); but q \lor \bot is not atomic, so we've got more work still to do.

What is h(q \lor \bot ), then? Well, we preserve the main connective and apply h to the components. So it's h(q) \lor h(\bot ). And we know h(q) = p \land q, so h(q \lor \bot ) = (p \land q) \lor h(\bot ). Putting this together with what we already knew, we're getting closer: now we have that h(p \land (q \lor \bot )) = q \land ((p \land q) \lor h(\bot )).

That just leaves h(\bot ) to sort out. Remember that \bot is compound, not atomic, so we still can't just apply h like any normal function; we need to stick to the plan. That is, we keep the main connective the same and apply h to all the components. In this case, the main connective of \bot is \bot , and there are no components. So h(\bot ) = \bot . (Remember Logicians' Weird Habit 2: we automatically count as succeeding at applying h to all the components: all zero of them.)

Putting this all together, we get our result: h(p \land (q \lor \bot )) = q \land ((p \land q) \lor \bot ). Note that it's not just the main connective of this sentence that stayed put: all the connectives in the sentence have stayed just as they were. In effect, we've just acted on the atomics inside the sentence, replacing each of them with whatever the substitution h says. That's the key idea of applying a substitution.
